channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Write It Up! How Fast Do Our Journalists Type?
item.description: When it comes to breaking news, it’s a race not only to get the story, but to record it. We tracked down some of the speediest fingers in our newsroom.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Oil Prices Tumble as Trump Shows Eagerness to Talk to Iran
item.description: Investors reacted to signals of possible de-escalation of the war in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: Iran Says ‘Non-Hostile’ Ships Can Sail Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ships with no ties to Israel or the United States would be allowed to pass, the government said, but it was unclear if any vessels would try.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Cargo ships in the Gulf, near the Strait of Hormuz, seen from the United Arab Emirates earlier this month.

item.title: New York Times Accuses Pentagon of Defying Court Order
item.description: The company said in a legal filing that the department sought to fashion an “end run” when it issued revised media rules on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times accused the Pentagon of adopting a fresh round of media restrictions in retaliation for an adverse ruling from a federal judge last week.

item.title: Delta Air Lines Says It Will Suspend Special Services for Congress Members
item.description: Airport escorts and “red coat” assistance for lawmakers will be suspended, the airline said on Tuesday, citing the extended partial government shutdown.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Stefani Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines said that it would suspend special services for members of Congress, amid a stalemate over funding for the Department of Homeland Security.

item.title: New Limits on Investors and a Debt Downgrade Add to Private Credit Woes
item.description: Investors are concerned about the health of the industry, which ballooned over the past decade.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Michael Arougheti, co-founder and chief executive of Ares Management, an investment firm that said Tuesday that 11.6 percent of investors in one of its funds had sought to redeem their shares.

item.title: High Oil and Gas Prices Could Outlast Trump’s War With Iran
item.description: While the president has promised rapid relief, Americans could feel the financial sting of the conflict for some time after it ends.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Even when the conflict ends, it may be weeks or months before American families and businesses see a true break in spiraling energy costs.

item.title: Epic Games Lays Off Over 1,000 Employees, Citing Fortnite Slump
item.description: The cut represents about 20 percent of the video game company’s work force, a spokeswoman said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Epic Games booth at the GDC Game Developers Conference in San Francisco in 2019. The company cited a “downturn in Fortnite engagement” starting last year when it announced layoffs on Tuesday.

item.title: Russian Oil Shipment Puts Focus on Kremlin Spy Outpost in Cuba
item.description: Moscow may be challenging President Trump’s effort to choke Cuba’s economy. China also has suspected listening posts on the island.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Norlys Perez/Reuters
item.media:description: Mr. Trump has spoken of using his economic leverage to mount a “friendly takeover” of Cuba and is pressing for the ouster of its president, Miguel Díaz-Canel.

item.title: Covid Relief Loans Are Haunting Small Businesses
item.description: The Small Business Administration lent $378 billion to keep businesses afloat. Getting paid back is proving difficult.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Anna Ottum for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Towns, a blueberry farmer in Alma, Ga., took out hundreds of thousands in federal loans during the pandemic.

item.title: War in Iran Is Disrupting Air Travel, Hitting Middle East Hardest
item.description: Some airlines and travelers, especially in the Middle East and elsewhere in Asia, are bearing the brunt of the pain, while others are doing better.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi, Pragati K.B. and Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hassan Ammar/Associated Press
item.media:description: A plane taking off from Beirut-Rafik Hariri International Airport in Lebanon last week, as smoke rose from the site of an Israeli airstrike.

item.title: Investors Question Whether Quick Peace in Iran Is Possible
item.description: Global markets are under pressure again amid doubts that the fighting in the Middle East can end anytime soon.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Investors and analysts have begun to question whether President Trump can find an off-ramp to war in the Middle East.

item.title: College Graduates Are Facing the Grimmest Job Market in Years
item.description: Artificial intelligence could reshape work, but for now a low-hire, low-fire labor market is the main impediment for young people seeking employment.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Marisa Chafetz for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I was hoping that at this stage, I would have something lined up,” said Erin Torres, who has been searching for a job for months.

item.title: Reality TV Confronts a Harsh TV Reality
item.description: The number of unscripted series has plummeted by a third since 2022. As the industry rapidly changes, an era is quietly vanishing.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin
item.media:credit: Guillem Casasus

item.title: Oil Prices Rise After Iran Denies Talks With Trump
item.description: President Trump had set off a drastic market reaction on Monday by backing away from a threat to strike Iranian energy infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times

item.title: Pentagon Adopts New Limits for Journalists After Court Loss
item.description: The Defense Department said it would close the Pentagon’s work area for journalists, among other changes, after a judge found the existing media policy unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: In October, the Pentagon adopted a policy that empowered it to declare journalists “security risks” and revoke their press passes if they engaged in any conduct that the Pentagon believed threatened national security.

item.title: ICE Agents Are Now Patrolling U.S. Airports. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The deployment comes as a battle over Department of Homeland Security funding has led to closed security checkpoints, long lines and missed flights.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Adam Gray/Reuters

item.title: Trump Faces Blowback on Easing Iran Oil Sanctions
item.description: President Trump once assailed the Obama administration for making cash payments to Iran. Now he supports sanctions relief that could give the country a $14 billion windfall.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and other Trump administration officials are struggling to explain the logic of lifting sanctions on Iranian oil.

item.title: Trump Sets Up ‘Pax Silica’ Fund to Reduce Global Dependencies
item.description: Trump officials said on Monday that the war in Iran had emphasized the need to reduce vulnerabilities for energy and technology.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Sheera Frenkel
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: The fund would build on the Trump administration’s efforts to shore up U.S. manufacturing of semiconductors.

item.title: For Western Oil Companies, War in Iran Means Bigger Profits, and Risks
item.description: American and European oil and gas companies are expected to earn a lot more as prices surge but are worried about the future.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Storage tanks in Stanton, Texas. Companies that operate in places like West Texas are earning a lot more for each barrel of oil they produce.

item.title: Warren Requests Information From MrBeast About Crypto and Children
item.description: The world’s most popular YouTube celebrity, who markets to children and has bragged about crypto trades, is getting into banking. Senator Elizabeth Warren has concerns.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber and Tara Siegel Bernard
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: China Eases Planned Increase to Gas Prices for 300 Million Drivers
item.description: In China, where half of new cars are electric vehicles or hybrids, a vast population still depends on gas. The government stepped in on Monday to “mitigate” the pain of surging costs.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maxim Shemetov/Reuters
item.media:description: A gas station in Beijing on Sunday. Even after the government set prices at the pump lower than planned, gas in China has jumped about 20 percent since the war in Iran began.

item.title: Markets Rally as Trump’s Iran Deadline Is Deferred
item.description: Stock futures rose and oil prices fell after President Trump cited “very good and productive” talks with Iran over ending the war.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: High gas prices continue to weigh on global markets, and President Trump.

item.title: Energy Attacks in War on Iran Could Turn Economic Shock Into Long-Term Damage
item.description: A new phase targeting oil and gas infrastructure in the Persian Gulf threatens to hurt businesses and customers around the world for months or even years.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hand-painted signs at a gas station in Samut Prakan, Thailand, last week said the station was out of diesel and 95-octane gasoline.

item.title: 5 Country Hotels Where You Can Celebrate Spring
item.description: From a high-design pavilion in upstate New York to a horse-farm-turned-hotel on a Swedish island, these new or renovated getaways revel in nature.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: How Epstein Helped Solve a Billionaire’s Problems With Women
item.description: The Wall Street titan Leon Black paid Jeffrey Epstein $170 million for what he said was tax and estate work. But his services went beyond that.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein, Jessica Silver-Greenberg, Steve Eder and David Enrich
item.media:credit: Illustration by Anna Kliewer; source images by Patrick T. Fallon/Bloomberg, Rick Friedman Photography/Corbis, via Getty Images.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: A ground stop was in effect early Monday as the Fire Department said it was responding to a runway incident. A New York Times journalist saw an Air Canada plane on the runway with a sheared-off nose.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack, Christine Chung, Karoun Demirjian and Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times

item.title: Stocks Bounce as Trump Backs Off Iran Infrastructure Threat
item.description: Oil prices tumbled and yields on government bonds pulled back from highs. But the response faded as the day went on.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Seth Wenig/Associated Press

item.title: Jeff Webb, Who Built a Competitive Cheerleading Empire, Dies at 76
item.description: Through Varsity Spirit, the company he established in 1974, he turned cheerleading into a multibillion-dollar juggernaut and exerted control over almost every aspect of it.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: A Window Into the Lives of New York City Renters
item.description: For Sunday’s issue of The New York Times Magazine, Gail Albert Halaban photographed city dwellers inside their apartments from across the street — with their permission, of course.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Gail Albert Halaban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Residents of Bedford-Stuyvesant in Brooklyn, photographed for a recent article in The New York Times Magazine.

item.title: Should I Tell My Employee I Think He Has ADHD?
item.description: Plus, my co-workers had an affair and misused company funds. Somehow, I’m the one dealing with the fallout.
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: This Corvette’s a Hybrid, but It’s Built for Raw Power, Not Efficiency
item.description: The ZR1X, starting at $207,000, is a Chevrolet showpiece that competes with rarefied rivals like Ferrari and McLaren at a fraction of their (stratospheric) prices.
item.dc:creator: Lawrence Ulrich
item.category: Chevrolet Division of General Motors Corp
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chevrolet Corvette ZR1X. For all the wonders of electric vehicles, sports-car fans have uncovered a dirty secret: Most aren’t particularly fun to drive. Enter the hybrid hypercars.

item.title: As War Disrupts India’s Gulf Ties, Economy Faces ‘New Broadside’
item.description: Generations of Indian workers and businesses have turned the Arab side of the Gulf into a vital appendage of their own country.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Giuseppe Cacace/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Migrant workers taking a break in Dubai this month.

item.title: News Outlets Pressure Pentagon to Restore Access After Court Ruling
item.description: A federal judge ruled on Friday that key parts of the Pentagon’s press policy were unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Brendan Smialowski/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Journalists leaving the Pentagon last year after news outlets declined to sign the Defense Department’s restrictive media rules.

item.title: CBS News Radio Crackles to a Close
item.description: A stalwart of the mass media century, the longstanding network that launched the careers of journalists like Edward R. Murrow will air its final broadcast in May.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Everett Collection/Alamy
item.media:description: The pioneering CBS journalists Edward R. Murrow, left, and William Shirer delivered riveting reports from Europe during World War II.

item.title: Amazon MGM Gets a Much-Needed Hit With ‘Project Hail Mary’
item.description: The movie, which stars Ryan Gosling, is on pace to be the company’s highest-grossing domestic film.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Jonathan Olley/Amazon MGM Studios, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Ryan Gosling stars in “Project Hail Mary,” which is based on a novel about the relationship between a reluctant hero and an alien.

item.title: Not All Malls Are Struggling
item.description: A certain type of shopping mall has become a surprising bright spot for real estate investors.
item.dc:creator: Ian Frisch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roosevelt Field, a mall in Uniondale, N.Y., earlier this week.

item.title: What to Know About Electric Cars When Gas Prices Are Surging
item.description: War in Iran has disrupted global oil supplies, which is prompting some car shoppers to look for ways to climb off “the gas-price roller coaster.”
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pre-owned electric vehicle dealership in North Hampton, N.H. There are exceptionally good deals to be had on used battery-powered cars.

item.title: War in Iran Reveals Strait of Hormuz’s Weakness as an Oil Bottleneck
item.description: But at just 35 miles wide, it did. It’s just the latest evidence of how dependent the global economy is on a handful of choke points.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Nielsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz, seen from Musandam, Oman.

item.title: Vertical Farms Tried to Compete With Open Field Farming. It Isn’t Going Well.
item.description: The industry was a darling of the venture capital world 10 years ago. With many farms out of business, the remaining companies have scaled back.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Madeleine Hordinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vertical farms, like the one operated by 80 Acres Farms in Florence, Ky., resemble sterile manufacturing facilities.

item.title: Laid Off in Midlife, China’s Reform Generation Braces for Downward Mobility
item.description: The future once seemed boundless for those who grew up during China’s reform era. Now in middle age, they are pinned between economic stagnation and institutional age discrimination.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:credit: Lisk Feng

item.title: U.S. Pauses Sanctions on Some Iranian Oil to Get More to Market
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent estimated that the move would add about 140 million barrels of crude to the oil market.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a post on X that Iran would see little economic benefit from the temporary removal of the sanctions.

item.title: Judge Rules Pentagon Restrictions on Press Are Unconstitutional
item.description: A federal judge tossed parts of the Pentagon’s restrictions on news outlets, saying they violated the First Amendment, in a lawsuit brought by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A federal judge ordered the press passes of seven journalists for The New York Times to be restored.

item.title: More! More! More! Tech Workers Max Out Their A.I. Use.
item.description: At a number of companies, employees compete on leaderboards to show how much A.I. they’re using. They’re racking up big bills along the way.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose
item.media:credit: Erik Carter

item.title: CBS News Radio, Onetime Home of Murrow, Will End Amid Layoffs
item.description: Bari Weiss, the editor in chief of CBS News, announced layoffs on Friday that affected more than 60 employees. The network is owned by the technology heir David Ellison.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Spencer Platt/Getty Images
item.media:description: The cuts are expected to affect several dozen journalists and crew members at numerous shows.

item.title: Fed Official Urges Caution on Rate Cuts as Iran War Drags On
item.description: Christopher J. Waller, a Federal Reserve governor, said he would support rate cuts later this year if the labor market continued to weaken.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters
item.media:description: Christopher Waller, left, warned that supply disruptions related to the Middle East conflict could stoke inflation.

item.title: The U.S. Economy Is Insulated From High Oil Prices. Americans Aren’t.
item.description: The overall economy has proved resilient in recent years, even as many households have struggled. The war with Iran is following the same pattern.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: Debris from an intercepted Iranian drone struck an oil site in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, on Saturday. The U.S.-led war with Iran is threatening to deliver another inflationary blow to American consumers.

item.title: The I.R.S. Is Phasing Out Paper Checks. Here’s How to Speed Your Refund.
item.description: President Trump has directed that most federal payments be issued electronically, including tax refunds. Last year nearly 10 million people got their refund via a paper check.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns
